chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Database Systems. (3-0). Credit 3. File structures and access methods; database modeling, design and user interface; components of database management systems; information storage and retrieval, query languages, high-level language interface with database systems. Prerequisites: CSCE 221 with a grade of C or better; junior or senior classification.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): CSCE 221 with a grade of C or better; junior or senior classification.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

• Explain the fundamental concepts of database systems, including data models, database architecture, and the role of database management systems (DBMS)
• Design and implement Entity-Relationship (ER) and Enhanced Entity-Relationship (EER) models to represent real-world data requirements
• Apply normalization theory and functional dependencies to design efficient and well-structured relational database schemas
• Write complex SQL queries to create, manipulate, and retrieve data from relational databases
• Evaluate and compare different database paradigms, including relational databases, NoSQL systems, and distributed databases
• Analyze data warehouse architectures and understand their application in business intelligence and decision support systems
• Implement database connectivity in applications and apply security principles to protect database systems

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
The grading will be on an absolute scale mentioned below:

Overall Grading Scale
| Rounded Total | Letter Grade |
|---|---|
| Total ≥ 90% | A |
| 80% ≤ Total < 90% | B |
| 70% ≤ Total < 80% | C |
| 60% ≤ Total < 70% | D |
| Total < 60% | F |

Component Weights

The grade is calculated based on the following course components:

Course Component Weights
| Component | Weight |
|---|---|
| Class Activities | 5% |
| Reading Quizzes | 15% |
| Homeworks | 35% |
| Midterm Exam | 20% |
| Final Exam | 25% |

chunks.title: Component Specific Policies
chunks.content:
Class Activities

• Class activities will be graded based on participation rather than correctness.
• They will only be available for completion during class time.
• In case of excused absences, the activity will be marked excused. Canvas processes excused absences by increasing the weight of the unexcused activities.
• Three unexcused absences are allowed during the semester. This will be processed at the end of the semester.

Homework

• Homeworks are individual assignments and must be completed.
• Collaboration with other students and the use of Al tools are strictly prohibited.

chunks.title: Regrade Requests
chunks.content: If you want to challenge any grading, you must do so within one week of grade release. Regrade period for assignments published after week 13 will be shorter and will be announced at the time of grade release. There may be no regrading period for the final exam.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

• Late submissions are only allowed for Homeworks.
• There will be 10% per day penalty for late submissions.
• There may be no late submission period for the final homework of the semester.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Weekly Topics
| Week | Topic |
|---|---|
| 1 | Course Introduction, Data models and elements of data models. |
| 2 | Data model types. File based data models. |
| 3 | Relational model basics |
| 4 | Normalization |
| 5 | SQL |
| 6 | Advanced SQL |
| 7 | Transactions |
| 8 | Review, Midterm exam |
| 9 | Spring break |
| 10 | Query optimization |
| 11 | Data warehouses |
| 12 | Distributed databases |
| 13 | NoSQL databases |
| 14 | More noSQL databases |
| 15 | Databases and security |
| 16 | Final Exam |

Homework Deadlines

Homeworks will be released on Monday. Typical deadline for the Homeworks will be Sunday night. Any change will be announced through canvas.

Exam Dates

• Midterm: Thursday, Mar 5, 2026. In class.
• Final Exam: April 30, 2026. 3:00p.m. – 5:00 p.m.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: Some homework problems and class activities will require use of Al to generate code. The scope of Al use will be defined in the problems. In principle, you may submit Al-generated code, or code that is based on or derived from Al-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in the comments: you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the response. Al tools may help you avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that the generated code is correct. It is your responsibility to identify errors in program logic through comprehensive, documented testing. Moreover, generated code, even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for improvement, in particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation.

chunks.title: Canvas
chunks.content: Dashboard for the entire course. This will link out to appropriate resources.

chunks.title: Campuswire
chunks.content:
• Campuswire will be the main forum to get help with homework and lab activities.
• You will be added to the discussion forum at the start of the semester.
• Whenever possible, post your questions publicly so that all students can benefit from it. Please do not post your code and solutions in public posts. Public posts can also be answered by other students so you should have a shorter response time for them.
• We will endorse good student responses.
• You have the option to post anonymously. Your post will only be anonymous to students. Instructors will be able to see your name and id on the posts.
• Please be respectful.
• Private messages can be posed to all instructors or an individual instructor.
• Private messages to individual instructors are more likely to be overlooked and should not be used. Send an email directly to the instructor.
• Posts directed to instructors that would benefit the entire class and should be visible to all students will be updated so that the post is visible by the entire class.

chunks.title: Google Drive
chunks.content: Google folder will be used to share lecture notes and in-class examples. You must be logged into your TAMU Google account to access these materials or submit Google Forms. Your TAMU Google account will have to be the default account in your browser, or you can open an incognito window and log in from there. It might take up to 24 hours after registering for the class to be able to access materials in the Google shared folder.

chunks.title: Zoom
chunks.content: Zoom will be used for online office hours, and meetings with instructors and teaching assistants. Links will be provided as needed.
